name: Bodhisattvas
shortDescription: Bodhisattvas are enlightened beings who have postponed their own nirvana to help others achieve enlightenment. They embody compassion and wisdom, striving to liberate all sentient beings from suffering.
description: In Buddhism, a Bodhisattva (Sanskrit: बोधिसत्त्व; Pali: बोधिसत्त) is an enlightened being who, out of compassion, has generated Bodhicitta, a spontaneous wish and a compassionate mind to attain Buddhahood for the benefit of all sentient beings. Bodhisattvas are a central figure in Mahayana Buddhism, where the path of the Bodhisattva is considered the ideal path to enlightenment. They vow to remain in the cycle of rebirth until all beings are liberated from suffering, embodying the virtues of wisdom (prajna) and compassion (karuna). The concept of the Bodhisattva is rooted in the idea that enlightenment is not merely a personal achievement but a universal responsibility. They cultivate the Six Perfections (Paramitas): Generosity (Dana), Morality (Sila), Patience (Kshanti), Effort (Virya), Concentration (Dhyana), and Wisdom (Prajna). Some famous Bodhisattvas include Avalokiteshvara (the embodiment of compassion), Manjushri (the embodiment of wisdom), and Vajrapani (the embodiment of power). Different Buddhist traditions emphasize different aspects of the Bodhisattva path, but the core principle remains the same: to attain Buddhahood for the sake of all beings. The Bodhisattva ideal is a cornerstone of Mahayana Buddhist ethics and practice, inspiring countless individuals to dedicate their lives to the service of others and the pursuit of enlightenment.
